title: Success Definition Audit
tagline: What did success mean to you ten years ago? What does it mean now — and what about the decade ahead, when the work itself will have moved?

children.text: Three rounds. Five factors. Eleven points to distribute in each round, across three points in time. The exercise is quiet on purpose — its job is to surface the shape your definition of success is already moving in, not to push you somewhere new.
Each round is fresh. Distribute the points as honestly as you can, then move to the next. The pattern of drift between the three rounds is the result.

Distribute eleven points across the five factors below based on what success meant to you ten years ago. Whole numbers. The total must add up to exactly eleven — which means you can't split evenly. That's deliberate.
Same five factors. Same eleven points. Distribute based on what success means to you now.

Imagine forward ten years. Some assumptions about your work that hold today may not hold then. The specific assumption worth holding lightly: that the work that defines you today is the work that will define you then. AI is reshaping what counts as a meaningful contribution from a senior leader — not everywhere, not evenly, but enough that the question is worth taking seriously.

Distribute the same eleven points across the same five factors — based on what you want success to mean to you in a future where meaningful parts of what you do today are done differently, or not at all, because the tools and the operating models will have moved.

pointAllocationFactors.label: Craft
pointAllocationFactors.description: Doing work that's genuinely good; mastery for its own sake.

pointAllocationFactors.label: Connection
pointAllocationFactors.description: Depth of relationships with people who matter.

children.text: Your success definition has moved less than most leaders' would across twenty years. Two possibilities, and only you can tell which. One: you settled on what mattered to you early and have been right ever since — entirely possible. Two: the changes happening around you, including the AI-era changes to senior leadership, haven't yet reached your definition of what counts. Worth sitting with the future round again to find out.

Unusual for money to rise across all three rounds. When it does, the question worth holding is whether you're treating financial security as a buffer against a future you suspect will be more turbulent than the past — and whether that hedge is shaping decisions you might otherwise make differently.

The most common late-career shift in the leaders I coach. The work itself becomes the thing — not what it gets you. There's a specific edge to this answer in an AI-era future: you're saying that doing the work well is what success will mean even when the question of what 'the work' is has moved. That's a meaningful answer, and it raises a sharper question worth holding lightly.

Often shows up in leaders who've spent ten years building something — a function, a business, a reputation — and are starting to notice the cost. In an AI-era framing, the rise of connection often reflects a quiet recognition: the parts of leadership AI can't substitute for are the human parts. Worth being explicit about which connections you'll actually invest in.

Frequently a marker of someone whose ladder has nearly run out of useful rungs. In an AI-era framing, contribution rising can mean two distinct things: you're orienting toward work whose value AI can't substitute for, which is a strong answer; or you're orienting away from the version of leadership you currently do because some of it is becoming uncertain, which is a different kind of answer. Worth knowing which.

Less common as a late-career shift. In an AI-era framing it's worth checking: is this a desire to be seen for what you've built — fair — or a desire to remain visibly the kind of senior leader you've been, in a moment when that role is changing? The first is fine. The second is worth being aware of.

Your success definition has moved across the three time-points. The specific shape of that movement — what's rising, what's falling, what (if anything) anchors you — is below. Read the chart first; the lines that matter are the ones in coral.

Money rising across all three rounds is unusual. When it shows up, the question worth holding is whether you're treating financial security as a buffer against a future you suspect will be more turbulent than the past. If yes, that's worth being explicit about — it changes how you make decisions about where you spend your remaining career attention, what you'll accept, what you'll decline. Worth checking: how much is enough? The answer is rarely "more."

Less common as a late-career shift, but real when it shows up. Worth checking: is this a healthy desire to be seen for what you've actually built, or a compensation for an earlier deprioritisation? Different questions, different answers. In an AI-era framing, recognition rising can also reflect a quiet anxiety about remaining visibly the kind of senior leader you've been, in a moment when that role is changing. If that's part of what's driving the shift, naming it helps.

The most common late-career shift in the people I coach. The work itself becomes the thing — not what it gets you. This is healthy, but it does require some active recalibration of what 'success' looks like to the people around you, who may still be operating on the version of you from ten years ago. In an AI-era framing, the sharper question is: which parts of your current craft are likely to remain over the next decade, and which parts is AI better positioned to do? Worth being honest with yourself about both. Craft rising is strongest when it's specific.

Often shows up in people who've spent ten years building something — a career, a business, a reputation — and are starting to notice the cost. Worth treating seriously. Connection that's been deprioritised for a decade doesn't restore itself by being valued more on a spreadsheet. The work is concrete: name the relationships that are actually load-bearing, and decide what you'll do this quarter to be present in them. The list is usually shorter than you'd expect.

Frequently a marker of someone whose ladder has nearly run out of useful rungs. The next decade is less about achieving more and more about what the achievement was for. This is a different kind of coaching conversation than the one you'd have had ten years ago. In an AI-era framing, contribution rising sometimes means you're orienting toward work whose value AI can't substitute for — which is a strong answer — and sometimes means you're orienting away from a version of leadership that's becoming uncertain. Worth being clear on which it is for you.

The most common drift across the people I coach. Worth checking: is this because you have enough, or because you've made peace with not having more? Both are fine answers. They're just different. The first is freedom; the second is acceptance. Either way, the implication is the same — you have more attention to spend on the factors that did rise, and the decisions you make from here should reflect that.

Recognition falling across the rounds is usually a quiet sign of maturity — you've stopped needing the room to see you to confirm what you know about yourself. Worth noticing, though: if you also see craft or contribution rising, this is healthy. If you see connection or money falling alongside it, the picture might be one of disengagement rather than confidence. Different conversation.

Craft falling across three rounds is unusual and worth holding closely. The possibilities: you're moving from a doer's role to a steward's role, and the work is becoming less about your hands — that's a healthy late-career shift. Or you're losing touch with what made you good at this in the first place, and the rest of the chart will tell you whether that's the picture. Worth being honest about which.

Connection falling across three rounds is the answer most people give that they're least comfortable looking at. Two cases. One: you've examined the connections that matter most to you and concluded that the time you give them is sufficient — fine. Two: it's drifted, you've noticed it drifting, and you keep telling yourself you'll get back to it. The second case is more common than the first. The work is concrete: which relationship needs an hour this week?

Contribution falling is usually one of two things. Either you've reached a point where the bigger contributions are coming from work you can't yet describe, and the falling weight is making room — that's fine. Or you're disengaging from the larger-than-yourself dimension of what you do, and the rest of the chart should tell you whether that's the picture. The questions to sit with: what were you contributing to ten years ago? Is that contribution still happening, by you or by someone else?

An anchor in money suggests either deep financial constraint, or money standing in for something else (security, freedom, control, leaving something behind for someone). In an AI-era framing it can also indicate a hedge against future uncertainty. Worth pulling that thread — the answer changes what kind of decisions you can make from here, and what kind you can't.

An anchor in recognition is unusual across three time-points and worth holding lightly. The question worth sitting with: what specifically do you want to be recognised for? Recognition that doesn't have a clear referent often persists because the underlying need it serves hasn't been named. Once it's named, the recognition itself usually becomes less load-bearing.

An anchor in craft is a strong answer — it suggests doing the work well has always been load-bearing for you, and you expect it to remain so. The work that follows from this anchor: be specific about what counts as craft for you, especially as the tools available change. Anchored craft is most valuable when the practitioner is honest about what's actually theirs and what's now better done with help.

An anchor in connection across three time-points is a meaningful answer. It implies that the relationships in your life have always mattered to your definition of success, and you expect them to continue to. Worth being explicit about which relationships, and what your share of the maintenance work is. Anchors that aren't named tend not to hold.

A factor that doesn't move across three decades is unusual and significant. If contribution is your anchor, it's worth being explicit about it. The work it implies — and the work you can decline — both follow from this being non-negotiable, including in a future where the work itself is reshaped. Anchors that stay quiet sometimes get crowded out by louder factors.

Your success definition has moved less than most leaders' would across twenty years. The most useful test: if you imagine someone you respect ten years from now describing what made their work meaningful, do they sound exactly like you do now? If yes, you may be right — some people do find what matters to them early and stay right about it. If you're not sure, it may be worth sitting with the future round again. The AI-era changes to senior leadership are landing differently across different roles and different careers; whether they should reach your definition of success is your call, but it's worth making the call explicitly rather than by default.
Want your three rounds, your rising and falling factors expanded, and three questions to sit with — as a PDF?
Or — if this surfaced something worth talking through — I do a free 30-minute conversation specifically for this kind of reflection. No agenda, no upsell.

seoTitle: The Shape of Your Success — fab.partners
seoDescription: A short, quiet exercise about what you're actually working toward — past, present, and a future where the work itself is moving. Three minutes.
